stage.name: Crypto in the Real World
stage.description: Tokenomics, Layer-2 rollups, governance systems, RWA tokenization, and institutional crypto adoption.

elements.content: Tokenomics
Supply mechanics, vesting schedules, and token design

1-4 years
Vesting

varies
Inflation

Supply Mechanics

Fixed supply (Bitcoin): 21M cap, deflationary — scarcity drives value narrative

Inflationary (ETH post-Merge): new issuance (~0.3% annually) offset by EIP-1559 burns — net deflationary when usage high

Elastic supply (AMPL): algorithmically adjusts supply to target $1 price — all wallets rebase proportionally

Burn mechanisms: token buyback-and-burn reduces supply — ties protocol revenue to token value

Token Distribution

VC allocation: 15-30% typical — check cliff + vesting to see when they can sell

Team allocation: 10-20% — vested over 2-4 years with 1-year cliff

Community/ecosystem: 40-60% — distributed via airdrops, mining, grants, treasury

Fully diluted valuation (FDV) = price × max supply — more meaningful than market cap for inflationary tokens

Token Utility Design

Governance: vote on protocol parameters (Uniswap UNI, Compound COMP) — 'valueless' if no fee sharing

Fee capture: protocol takes fee, distributes to stakers (dYdX, GMX) — real yield from real revenue

Work token: must stake to participate in network (Chainlink LINK, The Graph GRT) — demand tied to usage

Speculative premium: most token value is speculative future growth — utility alone rarely justifies price
Layer-2 Rollups
Optimistic and ZK rollups — scaling Ethereum

1,000-10,000
L2 TPS

90% cheaper
Cost

Optimistic Rollups

Execute transactions off-chain, post compressed state diff + transaction data to Ethereum L1

'Optimistic' = assume all transactions valid by default — no proof required for every tx

7-day fraud proof window: anyone can submit proof that a submitted batch contained invalid tx

Arbitrum, Optimism: largest L2s — $10B+ TVL, ~2000 TPS, gas 10-100× cheaper than L1

Sequencer centralization: single sequencer is current bottleneck — decentralization in progress

ZK Rollups

Post cryptographic validity proof (SNARK or STARK) with every batch — invalid tx mathematically impossible

Proof generation: computationally expensive off-chain, but verification cheap on L1 (<1M gas)

Instant finality: once proof verified on L1, funds can be withdrawn immediately (no 7-day window)

StarkNet (STARK), zkSync Era, Polygon zkEVM, Scroll — EVM compatibility is key challenge

ZK proof computation: 100-10,000× more compute than optimistic — improving rapidly with hardware acceleration

Data Availability

Rollup security requires L1 to store transaction data — without it, users can't reconstruct state if sequencer disappears

EIP-4844 (Proto-Danksharding, March 2024): new 'blob' transaction type — 128KB blobs at ~$0.01 vs $1+ calldata

Danksharding (future): full sharding with 64 blobs per block — 100,000+ TPS theoretical throughput

Volitions (hybrid): choose per-tx whether to store data on L1 (secure) or off-chain (cheap)
Governance Systems
On-chain voting, DAOs, and the limits of decentralization

4-10% typical
Quorum

$3B+ treasury
Largest DAO

DAO Mechanics

Token-weighted voting: 1 token = 1 vote — plutocracy risk, whale dominance

Delegation: transfer voting power without transferring tokens — increases participation

Quorum requirement: minimum % of tokens must vote for proposal to pass — prevents low-participation hijacks

Timelock: 2-7 day delay after proposal passes before execution — community can exit if unhappy

Governance Attacks

51% governance attack: acquire majority of governance tokens → pass proposal to drain treasury

Flash loan governance: borrow tokens, vote, repay — mitigated by Snapshot (off-chain) or requiring tokens before proposal

Voter apathy: most token holders don't vote — average participation <5% of supply on major DAOs

Slow and expensive: on-chain governance is slow (1-2 week cycles) — unsuitable for emergency responses

Governance Models

Optimistic governance: proposals execute unless vetoed in X days (inverse of normal voting)

Conviction voting (Gardens): voting power accumulates over time — rewards long-term holders

veTokenomics (Curve): vote-escrow locks tokens for up to 4 years — longer lock = more governance power

Multisig + governance: small multisig for emergency actions, governance for major parameter changes
Real-World Asset Tokenization
Bringing TradFi assets on-chain

$10B+
RWA TVL

5%+
T-bill yield

Tokenized Securities

US Treasury bills: Ondo Finance OUSG, Superstate — on-chain yield from off-chain T-bills

Process: fund buys T-bills, issues on-chain tokens, holders receive yield via rebasing or accrual

Advantage: composable DeFi yield — use T-bill tokens as collateral in Aave, earn yield while borrowing

Regulatory: securities law applies — KYC/AML required, only accredited investors in many jurisdictions

Real Estate & Commodities

Tokenized real estate: fractional ownership of properties — lower minimum investment, 24/7 liquidity

Gold (Paxos PAX Gold, Tether XAUT): each token = 1 troy ounce of allocated gold in vault

Carbon credits: on-chain carbon market — KlimaDAO, Toucan Protocol tokenize verified offsets

Royalty streams: music royalties, IP rights tokenized — Song Campaign, Royal — micro-invest in artist revenue

Infrastructure Challenges

Legal enforceability: token ≠ legal ownership in most jurisdictions — requires special purpose vehicle (SPV)

Oracle dependency: RWA prices come from off-chain sources — oracle manipulation risk remains

Redemption risk: if custodian goes bankrupt, token may not redeem for underlying asset

Liquidity: RWA tokens are illiquid — secondary market thin, bid-ask spreads wide
Institutional Crypto Adoption
ETFs, custody, and enterprise blockchain

$50B+
BTC ETF AUM

Jan 2024
Launch

Bitcoin ETFs

SEC approved spot Bitcoin ETFs in January 2024 — BlackRock IBIT, Fidelity FBTC, etc.

First-year AUM: ~$50B — fastest ETF launch in history by assets under management

Premium/discount: ETF trades close to NAV; AP (authorized participant) arbitrages any deviation

Tax implications: ETF gains taxed as securities; holding spot BTC has different tax treatment

Institutional Custody

Qualified custodian: legally defined role — must segregate assets, independent audits

Fireblocks, Copper, Anchorage: institutional-grade MPC custody — no single private key

Insurance: Lloyd's of London, Marsh offer crypto custody insurance — typically 1-3% of AUM annually

Staking custody: Coinbase Prime, Kiln — institutions earn staking rewards without managing validators

Enterprise Blockchain

Hyperledger Fabric: permissioned blockchain used by IBM, Maersk (TradeLens), HSBC for trade finance

JPM Coin: intra-JP Morgan settlement using permissioned Ethereum fork — real usage, closed ecosystem

Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDCs): 130+ countries exploring — wholesale vs retail CBDC designs

Enterprise Ethereum Alliance: 400+ members standardizing Ethereum for enterprise — EEA token standard
Regulatory Landscape
MiCA, SEC enforcement, and global crypto policy

EU June 2024
MiCA

Unclear
US status

EU: MiCA

Markets in Crypto-Assets (MiCA) regulation — effective June 2024 in full

Stablecoin issuers: must hold 1:1 reserves, publish monthly audit reports — Circle USDC compliant

CASPs (Crypto Asset Service Providers): must register in EU member state, passportable across EU

Prohibits algorithmic stablecoins (Terra-like) — must be asset-backed or e-money backed

US Regulation

SEC vs CFTC turf war: SEC claims most tokens are securities; CFTC claims BTC/ETH are commodities

Howey test: investment in common enterprise with expectation of profit from others' efforts = security

SEC enforcement actions: Ripple (XRP), Binance, Coinbase — using case law vs formal rulemaking

FIT21 bill (2024): attempts to define crypto asset classification — bipartisan support, uncertain Senate fate

Global Approaches

Singapore (MAS): crypto payment services licensed, no retail crypto ads — pragmatic approach

UAE (VARA): proactive regulation, crypto hub strategy — Binance, Bybit licensed

China: crypto trading banned since 2021 — mining banned 2021, CBDC (digital yuan) actively deployed

El Salvador: Bitcoin legal tender since 2021 — Chivo wallet, Bitcoin bonds, Volcano mining proposal
The Future of Web3
AI + blockchain, decentralized identity, and next-gen infrastructure

Emerging
AI + crypto

W3C standard
DID

AI & Blockchain

Bittensor: decentralized AI training network — miners earn TAO tokens for training ML models

Render Network: distributed GPU for AI rendering — users rent idle compute for tokens

Proof of inference: verifiable on-chain proofs that AI model ran correctly — ZK-ML emerging

Ocean Protocol: data marketplace — AI training data sold/bought on-chain with provenance tracking

Decentralized Identity

DIDs (W3C standard): decentralized identifiers — self-sovereign, no central authority

Verifiable credentials: cryptographically signed claims (e.g., 'over 18') stored in user wallet

ENS (Ethereum Name Service): .eth names as on-chain identity — composable across all dApps

Proof of humanity: on-chain verification of unique humans — Worldcoin iris scan, BrightID social graph

Infrastructure Evolution

Modular blockchains: separate execution, settlement, consensus, data availability layers — Celestia, Avail

Shared sequencers: multiple rollups use same sequencer — atomic composability across L2s

Full nodes in browsers: light clients with ZK proofs enable trustless verification without running node

Cross-chain communication: IBC (Cosmos), XCMP (Polkadot), Axelar — standardized blockchain interoperability

scenes.title: Knowledge Check — Crypto in the Real World
